flashcards.back: It states that for any states i,j, the n‑step transition probability satisfies P_{ij}^{(n+m)} = Σ_k P_{ik}^{(n)} P_{kj}^{(m)}, i.e., P^{(n+m)} = P^n·P^m.
A state i is recurrent if the probability of ever returning to i equals 1 and the expected return time is finite; otherwise it is transient (return probability < 1).
The period d(i) is the greatest common divisor of all n≥1 with p_{ii}^{(n)}>0; if d(i)=1 the state is aperiodic, meaning returns can occur at irregular intervals.
A stationary distribution π satisfies π = πP and Σ_i π_i = 1. For P = [[0.7,0.3],[0.2,0.8]], solving π = πP yields π = (2/5, 3/5).

flashcards.front: Irreducible Markov chain and communicating states
flashcards.back: Two states i and j communicate (i↔j) if each is reachable from the other; a chain is irreducible if every pair of states communicates.
An ergodic state is both positive recurrent (finite expected return time) and aperiodic, guaranteeing a unique stationary distribution and convergence to it.
In a cache with LRU, each possible ordering of recently used pages forms a state; a memory reference yields a transition probability for a hit or miss, enabling analysis of hit rates.

flashcards.front: M/M/1 queue as a Markov chain
flashcards.back: The number of customers in the system is the state; Poisson arrivals and exponential service give memoryless transitions, yielding a birth‑death process with rates λ and μ.
